source_metadata.title_raw: Lori Parlin - A-19-091 - August 7, 2019 - Shingle Springs

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 7, 2019

Lori Parlin
El Dorado County Supervisor
3971 Crosswood Drive
Shingle Springs, CA 95682

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-091

Dear Ms. Parlin:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

May you participate in the process to develop and vote to approve Community Design Standards applicable to the Shingle Springs Community Region, which are called for in General Plan Policy 2.4.1.2, in order to implement that policy, given that you own a home located within the Shingle Springs Community Region?

CONCLUSION

No. You are disqualified from participation because the decision on the Community Design Standards will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your interest in residential real property.

_______________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All statutory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-091
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

You were sworn in as the El Dorado County District IV Supervisor on January 7, 2019. You seek advice regarding a potential conflict of interest with the ongoing Shingle Springs Community Design Standards project due to the location of your personal residence.

You have been a resident of Shingle Springs since 2002 and reside within the Shingle Springs Community Region. You live on a fully developed parcel and have no other financial interests in any properties in the Shingle Springs Community Region.

In 2004, the El Dorado County Board of Supervisors adopted a new General Plan that included OBJECTIVE 2.4.1, which encourages community engagement in fostering and retaining the unique community identity of each of its five Community Regions including Shingle Springs. Policy 2.4.1.2 of OBJECTIVE 2.4.1 states that the County would develop design guidelines for each community with the community's input. As a resident, in 2012, you helped create the Shingle Springs Community Alliance (SSCA) to bring the community together to address County land use decisions. You were the President of SSCA until you stepped down from this position on December 11, 2018, after winning a seat on the El Dorado County Board of Supervisors.

After holding several community meetings with hundreds of participants in 2013 and 2014, SSCA decided to pursue implementation of Objective 2.4.1: COMMUNITY IDENTITY of the El Dorado County General Plan. The June 5, 2013 community meeting produced a set of survey results that shows the community's overwhelming support for railroad-themed Design Standards. On January 14, 2014, members of SSCA presented a Conceptual Overview of the Shingle Springs Community Plan, which includes Design Standards for commercial and multi-family parcels with development potential. The Design Standards do not affect single-family residential parcels like the one you own, nor fully-developed commercial or multi-family parcels.

The Conceptual Overview was based on community input from surveys and meetings. Due to concerns regarding the aesthetics of future development in the area, SSCA specifically chose to implement Policy 2.4.1.2 but asked for Design Standards which are more enforceable than Guidelines. This presentation was well received by the Board and ultimately led to an item on the Long-Range Planning Priority Matrix for planning purposes. Once the Design Standards process was included on the Priority Matrix, the community had done all it could and had to wait for the BOS to approve a budget item to hire a consultant to transform the survey results into Design Standards.

On June 14, 2017, SSCA members attended the monthly El Dorado County Community and Economic Development Advisory Committee (CEDAC) meeting, to collaborate on a letter of support for implementing the Community Design Standards/Guidelines Process. CEDAC submitted the letter to the BOS to support communities that wanted to fulfill Objective 2.4.1: COMMUNITY IDENTITY of the El Dorado County General Plan.

On December 5, 2017, the BOS approved funding to hire a consultant to work with the SSCA and 3 other Community Region groups to implement Policy 2.4.1.2, and create Design Guidelines and/or Standards. In 2018, several SSCA members, including yourself, and CEDAC Chairperson Kris Payne worked with County staff to write the Scope of Work for the Request for

File No. A-19-091
Page No. 3

Proposal (RFP) to hire a consultant. It is anticipated that the contract with the consultant will be
completed sometime in April 2019. At that time, the consultant will work with the Shingle Springs
community to formalize the community's 2013 survey results to create the Shingle Springs Design
Standards.

You now seek to participate in the process to develop and approve Community Design
Standards applicable to the Shingle Springs Community Region, which are called for in General
Plan Policy 2.4.1.2. The Community Design Standards will be approved as a separate document
from the General Plan and will not be adopted into the General Plan. No General Plan amendment
will be required for this project. Participating in the process would include, but not be limited to,
discussing the project and process with consultants, staff, and the public, and hearing and voting on
the item.

You own your residence, which is located within the Shingle Springs Community Region. It
is adjacent to, and within 500 feet of an undeveloped commercial parcel that would be affected by
the design standards. You also note that there are 8 undeveloped parcels within 1,000 feet of your
residence that could be affected by the Design Standards. You note that there are 1,430 residential
parcels in the Shingle Springs Community Region, and that 260 parcels are within 500 feet of an
undeveloped commercial parcel.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the decision's effect
on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) The financial interests that may give rise to an
official's disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are set forth in Section 87103 and include:

• An interest in any real property in which the official has an interest of $2,000 or more.
(Section 87103(b).) You have an interest in your residential real property.

Foreseeability and Materiality

A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the
financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official
or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Regarding financial interests not explicitly involved
in a decision, as here, a financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable.
In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than
hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected
absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).)

Real Property

Regulation 18702.2 provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on real property in which an official has a financial interest is material under
certain enumerated circumstances. The regulation provides that the effect is material whenever the

File No. A-19-091
Page No. 4

governmental decision "[i]nvolves property located 500 feet or less from the property line of the
parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any measurable
impact on the official's property..." (Regulation 18702.2(a)(7).)

Here, you note that the Community Design Standards would apply to commercial and multi-
family parcels with development potential. It is foreseeable that the standards will financially affect
your property, which is adjacent to and within 500 feet of one of the parcels that would be subject
to these Community Design Standards. Moreover, you have not presented clear and convincing
evidence that any specific plan-related decisions will not measurably impact your property. As
such, you have a disqualifying conflict of interest, unless the public generally exception applies.

Public Generally Exception

Commonly referred to as the "public generally" exception, Regulation 18703(a) permits a
public official to take part in a governmental decision that affects one or more of their interests if
the decision's financial effect on the interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public
generally. (See Section 87103.) In general, an effect on an official's interest is distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally if a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on
the official's interest is not unique when compared to the effect on the significant segment of the
public. (Regulation 18703(a).) A unique effect is defined to include a disproportionate effect on an
interest in real property resulting from the proximity of a project. (Regulation 18703(c).)

For purposes of the exception, 25-percent or more of all residential real properties within an
official's jurisdiction constitutes a "significant segment of the public." (Regulation 18703(b).) You
have indicated that there are 1,430 residential parcels in the Shingle Springs Community Region,
and that 260 parcels are within 500 feet of an undeveloped commercial parcel. While you have not
indicted the number of total residential parcels in the jurisdiction, the facts provided do indicate that
the number of parcels within 500 feet of an undeveloped commercial parcel is approximately 18-
percent of the residential parcels in the Shingle Springs Community Region, and that your property
is located adjacent to an undeveloped commercial parcel. Therefore, even if it can be established
that the significant segment threshold is met, the facts provided indicate that the decision will have
a unique effect on your property resulting from the proximity of the property to the project, and the
public generally exception does not apply.

Appearance as a Member of the General Public

Regulation 18704 sets forth the definitions of "making a decision," "participating in a
decision," and "using official position to attempt to influence a decision" for purposes of the Act.
Regulation 18704(d)(2)(A) establishes an exception to those definitions and provides as follows:

"(d) Exceptions. Making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision does not include: …

"(2) Appearances as a Member of the General Public. An appearance by a
public official as a member of the general public before an agency in the
course of its prescribed governmental function if the official is appearing
on matters related solely to the his or her personal interests, including
interests in: …

File No. A-19-091
Page No. 5

"(A) Real property owned entirely by the official, members of his or her
immediate family, or the official and members of his or her immediate
family[.]"

Under these provisions, you may appear as a member of the general public before the City
Council in the course of its prescribed governmental function if you are appearing on matters solely
related to your personal interests in your residence and if your residence is entirely owned by you,
members of your immediate family, or you and members of your immediate family.

To avail yourself of this exception, you must follow the procedures set forth in Regulation
18707. You must publicly identify each type of financial interest involved in the decision that gives
rise to the disqualifying conflict of interest. The identification must be made after the announcement
of the agenda item to be discussed or voted on, but before the discussion or vote commences. You
must also recuse yourself from voting on the matter, leave the dais to speak from the same area as
the members of the public and limit your remarks solely to your personal interests, and make clear
that you are not speaking in the interest of any other person or group.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:aja

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the decision's effect 
on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.)The financial interests that may give rise to an 
official's disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are set forth in Section 87103 and include: 
• An interest in any real property in which the official has an interest of $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b ).) You have an interest in your residential real property.

A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the

In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected

parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any measurable

Here, you note that the Community Design Standards would apply to commercial and multi-
family parcel.s with development potential. It is foreseeable that the standards will financially affect

official's jurisdiction constitutes a "significant segment of the public." (Regulation 18703(b ).) You 
have indicated that there are 1,430 residential parcels in the Shingle Springs Community Region, 
and that 260 parcels are within 500 feet of an undeveloped commercial parcel. While you have not 
indicted the number of total residential parcels in the jurisdiction, the facts provided do indicate that 
the number of parcels within 500 feet 9f an undeveloped commercial parcel is approximately 18-
percent of the residential parcels in the Shingle Springs Community Region, and that your property

that the significant segment threshold is met, the facts provided indicate that the decision will have

decision," and "using official position to attempt to influence a decision" for purposes of the Act. 
Regulation 18704( d)(2)(A) establishes an exception to those definitions and provides as follows:

governmental decision does not include: ... 
"(2) Appearances as a Member of the General Public. An appearance by a 
public official as a member of the general public before an agency in the 
course of its prescribed governmental function if the official is appearing 
on matters related solely to the his or her personal interests, including 
interests in: ...  
"(A) Real property owned entirely by the official, members of his or her 
immediate family, or the official"and members of his or her immediate 
family[.]" 
.
